items.q: In Pavlov's experiment, what is the conditioned response?
items.correct: Salivation to the bell alone, after pairing with food.
items.misconception: Salivation to the food (which is automatic).
items.random_wrong: The dog ringing the bell itself.
items.why: Students confuse the unconditioned response (automatic salivation to food) with the conditioned response (learned salivation to bell). The conditioned response is the LEARNED one.

items.q: Why is high IQ a problem for the statistical infrequency definition?
items.correct: It is statistically rare but not abnormal/undesirable.
items.misconception: Because high IQ is actually common.
items.why: Students miss that statistical infrequency lacks a value judgement - it just measures rarity. High IQ is rare but desirable, exposing the definition's limitation.

items.q: When is deception in research ever acceptable?
items.correct: When demand characteristics would otherwise invalidate the study, with thorough debrief and ethics approval.
items.why: Students think BPS guidelines forbid deception outright. They actually permit it when justified, minimised, and followed by full debrief allowing withdrawal of data.

items.q: Top-down offender profiling was developed by:
items.why: It's FBI. “Canter” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Differential association theory was proposed by:
items.why: Correct answer: Sutherland. “Eysenck” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Lombroso publish his atavistic form theory?
items.why: The real figure is 1876 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
